summary: Cathy O'Neil previews her participation in a Joint Math Meetings panel about the public face of mathematics. She distinguishes her work from other panelists who explain math to the public, noting that she instead focuses on critiquing mathematical culture and calling out the misuse of math's authority. She specifically targets how public trust in mathematics gets exploited to lend false objectivity to political agendas, citing Bill Gates as an example. The post solicits reader questions for the panel moderator.
key_points: O'Neil sees her role as critiquing math culture rather than explaining math concepts to the public
Public trust in mathematics is being exploited to give political campaigns a veneer of objectivity
Mathematical modeling is often presented as objective when it is not
She focuses on issues like women in math and the misapplication of mathematical authority
There's a distinction between teaching math and calling out its misuse
money_quotes: I specialize in calling out the misapplication of mathematical imprimatur.
The public's trust in mathematics and mathematician is being perverted into a political campaign about the (supposed) objectivity of mathematical modeling.
I don't spend too much time explaining math to the general public.
key_insight: The public's trust in mathematics is being weaponized to make political arguments appear objective, and someone needs to call this out.
